content:
{
  "title": "Role-Aligned Prompting for Enhanced Code Generation in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Current prompting methods often yield code that lacks alignment with the intended functional role or context within a larger software system. This leads to generated code that is less reusable, maintainable, or directly integrable.",
  "motivation": "Modern software requires components that adhere to specific architectural roles (e.g., controllers, services, utilities). LLMs typically generate code snippets in isolation, ignoring these contextual requirements. Prompting approaches that explicitly encode intended role can bridge this gap, enhancing both code quality and practical utility.",
  "method": "We propose 'role-aligned prompting,' wherein prompts are augmented with concise, explicit descriptions of the code's intended architectural role (e.g., 'Implement as a model class for...') using a standardized schema. The method involves: (1) designing a role descriptor taxonomy, (2) appending these descriptors to prompts, and (3) optionally conditioning generation with an explicit 'role' token. We hypothesize that this guides the LLM to produce code that better fits real-world software contexts. The method will be compatible with both in-context and chain-of-thought prompting.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on HumanEval, MBPP, and a curated set of real-world GitHub tasks annotated with role descriptors. Baselines include standard prompts and recent context-aware prompting techniques. Metrics focus on functional correctness, role adherence (measured by human and automated code review), and integration success in sample applications."
}
